bio.photo_notes: Portrait of John Redpath Sr. (1796-1869), Scottish-Canadian sugar baron and industrial pioneer.

burial.plot_notes: Cimetière Mont-Royal Montreal — patriarch of the Canadian Redpath family.

notable.notable_blurb: Founded Canada's first sugar refinery 1854, canal builder

parents.father_research_notes: Orphaned young; lived with half-sister Elspeth Redpath Fairbairn after parents' deaths. Not built per Jalapeño Rule.
parents.mother_research_notes: Elizabeth Pringle Redpath; possibly aunt of Margaret Pringle (wife of George Drummond, John's master in Edinburgh). Connection unverified.

career.notes: Apprenticed as stonemason in Edinburgh under George Drummond — expert stone mason, successful building contractor, Edinburgh City Council member. Fellow apprentice was Drummond's brother Robert.

Came to Canada 1816 under British Government's emigration scheme to employ returning veterans from the Napoleonic Wars. Worked as a mason on the Lachine Canal (Montreal) and the Rideau Canal (Ottawa) — two of British North America's most important early infrastructure projects.

Built Canada's first sugar refinery in 1854, importing sugar cane from the West Indies on his own ships. The company became Redpath Sugar — still operating today, now owned by American Sugar Refining.
Active in banking, real estate development, and Montreal politics. Helped pioneer the industrial movement that made Montreal the largest and most prosperous city in Canada in the 19th century.
Supported missions, colleges, and hospitals. His son Peter (from second marriage to Jane Drummond) later financed the construction of McGill's Redpath Museum and Redpath Library.

landmarks.name: Redpath Sugar Refinery (Toronto, 1958)
landmarks.type: industrial successor of his 1854 Montreal refinery
landmarks.notes: Modern continuation of Canada Sugar Refining Company which he founded in Montreal in 1854. The Toronto facility opened 1958 (89 years after his death) and houses the Redpath Sugar Museum.

narrative_blocks.header: Stonemason apprentice to Canada Sugar Refining founder
narrative_blocks.body: John Redpath was orphaned young and apprenticed as a stonemason in Edinburgh under George Drummond. In 1816, at age twenty, he sailed to Canada under the British Government's post-Napoleonic-War emigration scheme, settling in Montreal with his brother Robert and nephew Peter — all stonemasons. He worked as a mason on the Lachine Canal and Rideau Canal — two of British North America's most consequential early infrastructure projects.

narrative_blocks.header: Built Canada's first sugar refinery in 1854
narrative_blocks.body: In 1854 Redpath built Canada's first sugar refinery — the Canada Sugar Refining Company in Montreal — importing sugar cane from the West Indies on his own ships. The company became Redpath Sugar, which still operates today and is now owned by American Sugar Refining. He also held interests in banking, real estate, and Montreal politics, helping to pioneer the industrial movement that made Montreal the largest and most prosperous city in Canada in the nineteenth century.

narrative_blocks.header: Two marriages, philanthropy through McGill son
narrative_blocks.body: John Redpath married twice: first to Janet McPhee in 1818, who died in 1834 the year their son John James was born; then in 1835 to Jane Drummond of Edinburgh, who survived him by thirty-eight years and died in 1907 at age ninety-one. His son Peter Redpath, from the second marriage, financed the construction of McGill University's Redpath Museum and Redpath Library — securing the family's place in Canadian academic philanthropy. John Redpath himself supported missions, colleges, and hospitals throughout his life.

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of John James Redpath (1834-1884), Redpath Sugar partner.

burial.plot_notes: Cimetière Mont-Royal Montreal — Redpath family grave.

notable.notable_blurb: Redpath Sugar dynasty partner, Canada Sugar Refining

parents.mother_research_notes: Janet McPhee (1791 Stormont Glengarry Upper Canada - Jul 26, 1834 Montreal age 43). John Redpath Sr.'s first wife. Died the year John James was born — likely childbirth-related complications. Not built as separate entry per Jalapeño Rule.

career.organization: John Redpath and Son (later Canada Sugar Refining Company)
career.notes: Made partner alongside brother George Drummond Redpath by contract registered July 4, 1862 when their father John withdrew from the business. Partnership ended December 31, 1867; Peter Redpath + Drummond continued alone, joined by Francis Robert Redpath in 1872.

naming_inspiration.description: First name John honors his father John Redpath Sr., the Scottish-born founder of Canada Sugar Refining Company.

narrative_blocks.header: Redpath Sugar partner, died at fifty
narrative_blocks.body: John James Redpath was made a partner in John Redpath and Son alongside his brother George Drummond in 1862 when their father withdrew from the business. The partnership lasted five years before being dissolved December 31, 1867 — months after John James's 1867 marriage to Ada Maria Mills at Putney England. He died June 4, 1884 at Montreal at age fifty, seventeen years before his wife and son would die in the Redpath Mansion shooting.

narrative_blocks.header: His family built Canada's first sugar refinery
narrative_blocks.body: John James was a son of John Redpath, founder of the Canada Sugar Refining Company, whose Montreal works gave the country its first sugar refinery. The Redpath name still appears on Canadian sugar today.

narrative_blocks.header: His widow died in the Redpath Mansion shooting
narrative_blocks.body: After his death at fifty, his wife Ada was widowed for seventeen years. She and their son Clifford died together in the unexplained 1901 Redpath Mansion shooting.

notable.notable_blurb: Died with son in famous Redpath Mansion shooting

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law; Founded Canada's first sugar refinery 1854

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Ada Maria Mills Redpath (1842-1901).

burial.plot_notes: Cimetière Mont-Royal Montreal — Redpath family grave; buried Jun 15, 1901 with her son Jocelyn Clifford who died alongside her.

narrative_blocks.header: Marriage contract gave her control of her assets
narrative_blocks.body: Ada married John James Redpath in 1867 at Putney, England — an unusual cross-Atlantic ceremony for the Mills and Redpath families. Her marriage contract stipulated she would control her own assets as if no marriage had taken place — a striking provision for 1867. In November 1870 she purchased the family villa at 1065 Sherbrooke Street Montreal, which would become the site of her death thirty-one years later.

narrative_blocks.header: Died with son in famous Redpath Mansion shooting
narrative_blocks.body: On June 13, 1901 at six in the evening at the Redpath Mansion 1065 Sherbrooke Street Montreal, two revolver shots were heard from Ada's bedroom. Servants found her dead and her twenty-four-year-old son Jocelyn Clifford fatally wounded. The case attracted wide publicity given the Redpaths' Canadian Sugar Refining fortune, but the coroner's inquest was perfunctory; the prevailing theory held Ada had suicidal intent and Clifford was accidentally shot trying to take the revolver from her.

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Amy Linda Redpath Roddick (1868-1954).

burial.plot_notes: Cimetière Mont-Royal Montreal — Redpath family grave.

cross_connections.link_text: Designed Roddick Gates for Amy's 1924 donation
cross_connections.display_label: Lt. Grattan Dalrymple Thompson — Roddick Gates designer

narrative_blocks.header: Family matriarch at thirty-eight, never married before
narrative_blocks.body: Amy Linda Redpath spent her infancy in Europe and grew up as the eldest child and only daughter of Ada Maria Mills Redpath and John James Redpath. She became the family matriarch — managing the household, hiring staff, paying bills, tending her ailing mother through years of decline, and even rewriting her brother Clifford's law lectures to help him study for exams. She did not marry until age thirty-eight, in 1906, to the distinguished McGill surgeon Sir Thomas George Roddick.

narrative_blocks.header: Roddick Gates donation memorialized husband at McGill
narrative_blocks.body: In 1924 Amy commissioned and donated the iconic Roddick Gates at McGill University to commemorate her late husband Sir Thomas George Roddick. The gates were designed by her sister Elizabeth Grace Redpath's husband Lt. Grattan Dalrymple Thompson — an unusual within-family architectural collaboration. On her death in 1954 she bequeathed one hundred twenty thousand dollars to the Redpath Library and was buried in the family grave at Mount Royal Cemetery.

education.notes: Studied sciences at McGill University. Was too ill to work for any length of time due to chronic illness.

narrative_blocks.header: Witness to mother's death, dead within a year
narrative_blocks.body: Peter Whiteford Redpath was the eldest son of Ada Maria Mills Redpath and John James Redpath. He told the story of his mother's death — he heard the shots from his mother's bedroom on June 13, 1901, dashed in, and found her and his younger brother Clifford on the floor. Peter himself died the following year, 1902, at age thirty-two or thirty-three.

narrative_blocks.header: Tuberculosis cure across continents, died California
narrative_blocks.body: Peter Whiteford Redpath studied sciences at McGill but was too ill to work, declining a position his uncle offered at the family sugar mill. He spent long periods pursuing rest cures for tuberculosis in Ste-Agathe-des-Monts in the Laurentians, upper New York State, Colorado, and finally California. After his mother's death in June 1901 his health rapidly deteriorated; his sister Amy accompanied him by train to Redlands California where he died of tuberculosis May 18, 1902, his body returned to Montreal for burial at Mount Royal.

burial.plot_notes: Ross Bay Cemetery Victoria BC — moved west from Montreal in later life.

narrative_blocks.header: Moved west to British Columbia in later life
narrative_blocks.body: John Reginald 'Regy' Redpath married Florence Amelia Botterell in 1907 and had three sons born in Alberta and Quebec. He eventually moved west to Oak Bay near Victoria British Columbia where he died at age eighty in November 1951. He was buried at Ross Bay Cemetery Victoria — outside the Mount Royal family burial pattern that held for his siblings and parents.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name Mills carries the maternal grandfather's surname — John Easton Mills, the Mayor of Montreal who died of typhus 1847. Naming honors the Mills civic legacy across the Redpath generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Bonnie Brae summer farm, two marriages, four children
narrative_blocks.body: Harold Mills Redpath married Alice Graham Watson in July 1897 and moved with her to 355 Olivier Avenue Westmount; in 1901 he purchased the Merriman farm in Georgetown Quebec and renamed it Bonnie Brae, where the family summered regularly through 1910. He and Alice had four children — Elizabeth Grace, John, Peter Harold, and Hugh — before separating; he later married Marie Adele Prendiville. He died at Saanich near Victoria British Columbia on December 26, 1948 at age seventy-five.

burial.plot_notes: Cimetière Mont-Royal Montreal — Redpath family grave; buried with his mother June 15, 1901.

notable.notable_blurb: Lost in the 1901 Redpath Mansion mystery

education.notes: Graduated McGill in law shortly before his death at 24 in the Redpath Mansion shooting.

narrative_blocks.header: He had just earned his law degree
narrative_blocks.body: Jocelyn Clifford Redpath, youngest of the Montreal sugar-refining family's five children, had only lately graduated in law from McGill University. He was twenty-four and, by the accounts of the day, in good health and high spirits.

narrative_blocks.header: He died in the 1901 Redpath Mansion mystery
narrative_blocks.body: Redpath and his mother Ada died together at the family mansion in June 1901, in a tragedy that has never been fully explained. A coroner's inquest reached a verdict, but the case remains one of Montreal's enduring mysteries. He was buried beside his mother at Mount Royal Cemetery.

burialCemetery.name: Cimetière Mont-Royal
burialCemetery.notes: Cimetière Mont-Royal (Mount Royal Cemetery), Outremont, Montreal QC. Major Protestant cemetery founded 1852 on the slopes of Mount Royal. Burial site of multiple members of the Lyman family — Hannah Stiles Lyman's descendants who became prominent Montreal druggists, lawyers, and civic figures: Elisha Lyman Jr. (with his three wives memorialized together), Henry Lyman, Mary Corse Lyman, Henry Herbert Lyman (later commemorated here after death on the Empress of Ireland 1914), Albert Clarence Lyman, Frederick Stiles Lyman, Edith Mary Louise Lyman, and others.
